text: seem appropriate .” With a heavy, shaky sigh, she lowered the gun, pointing the barrel at the floor. “What are you doing to me? I can’t even stay mad at you.” She averted her gaze, head bowed . “That because I’m back a day early ?” She smirked and met his optics. “Maybe. Couldn’t stay away , huh ?” Ronin couldn’t identify the gleam in her eyes, but it added vibrancy to her face . “Apparently not,” he replied. The truth of it struck him, hard; he couldn’t stay away from her. After arguing over the length of his trip, claiming three days was barely enough, he’d been the one to cut it short . He dropped his pack on the floor beside the rifle. It landed with a heavy, metallic clang, and a small cloud of dust rose from the fabric . “You weren’t kidding when you said the Dust stays with you .” “It isn’t the only thing in the world, but it permeates everything .” “I still don’t know what the hell you’re talking about most of the time,” she said, a corner of her mouth lifting. Teasing . Was that the right word? “Go on and clean yourself up .” “ Lara — ” “ Not yet .” “I have to — ” “Go clean up, and then you can tell me all the details. I’ve been bored out of my fucking skull since you left, and now my fingertips are sore, on top of it .” She rose lithely, stretched, and walked up the stairs. Ronin watched the gentle sway of her hips from below. He should demand she stop and listen to him . Instead, he followed her up. She strolled into his room, directly toward his bed, and Ronin froze. What was she doing? Why, given what he needed to tell her, was his desire for her amplifying ? Lara stopped in front of the chest, set the pistol down, and returned to the hallway. “It’s not very good, but I made you something. It’s on your bed.” She raised her fingers to show him the tiny cuts on their tips. “I bled for it, so you’d better at least pretend to appreciate it .” He turned his head as she walked past him, following her progress toward her room, unable to form a coherent sentence. Why couldn’t he just tell her ? Death was part of the world; it always had been, and always would be. How many people, whether flesh or metal, had he seen, stilled forever? It was the wrong question to ask himself . Tabitha and the synth made 115,299, at least since his awakening. Many of them were already dead or deactivated long before Ronin found them, but they remained in his memory, constant reminders of the world’s unforgiving nature . He knew there were more — millions of them — buried in the Dust, hidden in the ruins. He knew there were more, from before the Black Out, trapped in his inaccessible memories . Entering his bedroom, he turned on the light and undressed. His fingers were stiff as he worked the zippers and buttons, and his optics wandered — first to the pistol on the chest, and then to the bed. The blankets were rumpled, as though Lara had been atop them while he was gone. Spread atop one of the pillows was a shirt, made from the gray fabric he’d given her before his departure . Ronin brushed his hand over the dimples in the bedding. What had she looked like in his bed? How might it have felt, to be in it with her? His processors could composite her image into the scene, but such could never compare to reality . No; not now, not while he was covered in Dust and carrying such devastating news . He stepped into the adjoined bathroom and showered, using a cloth to wipe away the dirt from his skin. Steam billowed around him as he scrubbed beneath his finger nails. His sensors registered the water temperature — one hundred and ten degrees Fahrenheit — but it was just another number. A few weeks ago, Tabitha would’ve been just another number, too. One hundred and fifteen thousand, two hundred ninety-eight. That was before Lara . Lara was the key. The reason Tabitha was more than a number, more than a nameless face. Because of Lara, Tabitha was a person, and she had loved someone and been loved in return. Her death meant something . Every death did, whether he understood it or not . Turning off the water, he stepped out of the tub, dabbing moisture from his skin with a towel. He stopped in front of the mirror and wiped away the condensation. His face was unchanged. No indication of grief, no sign of the hardships the Dust had wrought upon him . He walked into the bedroom and pulled on a clean pair of pants before picking up the shirt Lara had made; it sported inconsistent stitching, uneven cuts, and a tiny stain near the left shoulder that was likely her blood. Somehow, those flaws made the shirt more appealing . “So, tell me,” Lara said . Ronin turned his head to see her walk in through the open door. Her eyes ran over his bare torso before focusing on his optics. She sat on the edge of the bed . “Your bag looked full. You find anything good ?” “I did,” he replied; he hated that it was the truth. “What’s in the bag doesn’t matter , Lara .” “So…don’t keep me in suspense! Tell me. I don’t think I’ve ever seen you so serious, which says a lot. Not like you had much of a sense of humor to begin with .” He folded the shirt and laid it atop the chest, moving to stand in front of her.
